topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing into my eyes as I stood before the canvas. It was *my* piece, the culmination of six months of sleepless nights and raw emotion, now hanging there, bathed in a spotlight I hadn't asked for. A knot tightened in my stomach, cold and hard. I took a step forward, my jaw clenching so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice thin and brittle, trying to project authority I absolutely did not feel. The curator, smiling with that practiced, vacant pleasantness, merely offered a tight, dismissive wave. I wanted to stride over, to snatch the frame, to reclaim the space where my vision was being paraded as someone else's acquisition. My hands were shaking, and I forced them to remain still, a desperate attempt to keep the tremor from showing on my face.

[story 2]

I found the photograph tucked into the corner of the catalogue, the one showing my portrait hanging next to some abstract nonsense. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air. It was a deliberate placement, an assertion of ownership that bypassed every boundary I had set. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I slammed the catalogue shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the hushed room. "This is unacceptable," I bit out, the words tasting like ash. I could feel the blood pounding in my temples, a furious rhythm against my skull. I wanted to turn around and walk out, to let the silence of the street swallow the gall of this whole situation. Instead, I forced myself to look at the artwork, cataloging every detail with a cold, sharp assessment, a silent vow to make this display a monumental mistake.

[story 3]

The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, suffocating. I watched as a group of patrons drifted around the exhibition, their murmurs sounding like an irritating swarm of flies. My focus narrowed entirely onto the painting—my painting—and a slow, building heat began to spread through my chest. I clenched my fists inside my coat pockets until my knuckles were white, the pressure a physical manifestation of the churning frustration inside me. When the gallery assistant approached, offering a rehearsed explanation, I didn't look at them. I just stared past them, my gaze fixed on a distant point, radiating a palpable sense of utter displeasure. "You need to remove it," I stated, my voice dangerously level, every syllable weighted with an unyielding demand. The silence that followed was heavy, expectant, and entirely mine.
